sections.title: Overview
sections.content: BPPV causes brief position-triggered vertigo and is often manageable in conservative care, but central vertigo, stroke, severe headache, new hearing loss, or persistent spontaneous symptoms require caution and escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Brief spinning episodes triggered by rolling in bed, looking up, or bending
- Nausea and imbalance
- Symptom-free periods between position changes
- Fear of falls or avoidance of head movement

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Do not assume BPPV if symptoms are continuous, the patient has focal neurological findings, severe headache, acute hearing loss, or strong stroke risk features
- Positional testing should only be used when the presentation fits and the clinician is trained
- Falls risk can remain significant even when the diagnosis is benign

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen stroke and neurological red flags, hearing change, and whether positional symptoms are brief and reproducible
- Stop and escalate if the pattern is atypical or the patient becomes unsafe
- Provide falls prevention advice and supervision with transfers if the patient is unsteady

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- If trained and appropriate, perform positional testing and consider canal-specific repositioning manoeuvres
- Reassess symptom response and gait safety after treatment
- Refer for medical or vestibular review if symptoms are atypical, persistent, or repeatedly recurrent

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The safest BPPV clinician is one who recognises when the presentation does not behave like BPPV
